title: Strahd von Zarovich
summary: The vampire lord of Barovia and Darklord of Ravenloft, Strahd is one of D&D's most iconic and tragic villains.
content:
## Strahd von Zarovich

**Title/Role:** Darklord of Barovia, Count of Castle Ravenloft
**Setting:** Ravenloft (Domains of Dread)
**Race/Species:** Human (Vampire)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard/Fighter
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Count Strahd von Zarovich is the first vampire and the eternal ruler of the demiplane of Barovia, a mist-shrouded valley trapped within the Domains of Dread. Once a mortal conqueror and military genius, Strahd made a pact with the Dark Powers to claim immortality and the love of Tatyana Federovna, a young woman who reminded him of everything his ruthless life had denied him. When Tatyana chose his younger brother Sergei instead, Strahd murdered Sergei on their wedding day and drank his blood, completing his transformation into a vampire. Tatyana threw herself from the castle walls rather than submit to Strahd, and the Dark Powers sealed Barovia in mist as Strahd's eternal prison.

Strahd is not a mindless monster. He is cultured, intelligent, and devastatingly charming. He views himself as a tragic figure worthy of sympathy, yet he is utterly incapable of genuine love or compassion. He is a master strategist who treats adventurers entering his domain as entertainment — testing them, toying with them, and ultimately destroying them. His castle, Ravenloft, is a labyrinthine fortress filled with traps, undead servants, and dark secrets.

What makes Strahd enduringly compelling is the tension between his sophistication and his monstrousness. He can quote poetry and play the organ one moment, then drain the life from a peasant the next. He is eternally tormented by his inability to possess Tatyana's soul, which reincarnates in Barovia across the centuries, always dying before Strahd can claim her. He is both the villain and the prisoner of his own story.

### Notable Appearances

- **I6: Ravenloft (1983)** — The original adventure module by Tracy and Laura Hickman, widely considered one of the greatest D&D adventures ever written. Introduced Strahd and the card-reading mechanic that randomizes key elements of the adventure.
- **Ravenloft Campaign Setting (1990, 1994, 2001)** — Expanded Barovia into the larger Domains of Dread, with Strahd as the most prominent Darklord.
- **Curse of Strahd (2016)** — The acclaimed 5th Edition reimagining of the original module, widely regarded as one of the best 5e hardcover adventures. Expanded the sandbox elements and deepened Strahd's characterization.
- **Van Richten's Guide to Ravenloft (2021)** — Further expanded the Domains of Dread with updated lore on Strahd and Barovia.
- **I, Strahd: The Memoirs of a Vampire (1993)** — Novel by P.N. Elrod told from Strahd's perspective.

### Using in Your Campaign

Strahd works best when he is ever-present but not immediately confrontable. He should appear early to demonstrate his power and personality, then withdraw to let the party explore Barovia and gather allies and artifacts. Use him as a recurring presence who watches through his spies (wolves, bats, Vistani), sends taunting invitations, and occasionally intervenes to remind the party of his dominance. The key to running Strahd is to make him feel like a real person with desires and frustrations, not just a stat block. Let him monologue, let him be charming, and let the horror come from how quickly that charm turns to cruelty.

title: Acererak
summary: A demilich of terrifying power, Acererak is the architect of the infamous Tomb of Horrors and one of D&D's deadliest villains.
content:
## Acererak

**Title/Role:** Demilich, Archlich, Creator of the Tomb of Horrors
**Setting:** Greyhawk (originally), Multiverse
**Race/Species:** Human (Demilich/Lich)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil

### Overview

Acererak is the cambion son of the demon lord Orcus and a human woman, though he transcended his origins to become one of the most powerful undead beings in the multiverse. As a mortal wizard of prodigious talent and boundless cruelty, he pursued lichdom and then something beyond — the state of a demilich, in which his soul departed his physical remains entirely, leaving behind only a jewel-encrusted skull of devastating power.

Acererak is best known as the creator of the Tomb of Horrors, an elaborate deathtrap dungeon designed to lure adventurers to their doom so that Acererak can harvest their souls. The tomb is filled with devious traps, misleading clues, and instant-death hazards that have been killing player characters since 1975. Acererak does not build these tombs out of paranoia or defense — he builds them as art, as expressions of his contempt for mortal life, and as engines of soul collection.

Unlike many villains who seek conquest or destruction, Acererak's goals are more abstract and terrifying. He seeks to consume enough souls to achieve true godhood, or perhaps to unravel the fabric of reality itself. He builds tomb after tomb across the planes, each one a masterwork of lethal architecture, and watches with amusement as heroes and fools alike throw themselves into his gauntlets.

### Notable Appearances

- **S1: Tomb of Horrors (1978)** — Gary Gygax's legendary adventure module, originally designed as a tournament dungeon to challenge the best players. Acererak waits at the end as a demilich.
- **Return to the Tomb of Horrors (1998)** — Bruce Cordell's expanded adventure revealing Acererak's larger plans and his fortress in the Negative Energy Plane.
- **Tomb of Annihilation (2017)** — The 5th Edition adventure set in Chult, where Acererak has built the Soulmonger beneath an ancient tomb to harvest souls on a massive scale. Revealed Acererak as an active, scheming villain rather than a passive dungeon guardian.
- **Tales from the Yawning Portal (2017)** — Includes an updated version of the original Tomb of Horrors.
- **Ready Player One (2011/2018)** — Acererak appears in Ernest Cline's novel and its film adaptation, introducing him to a wider pop culture audience.

### Using in Your Campaign

Acererak is best used as a distant, cosmic-level threat whose influence is felt through his creations rather than direct confrontation. His tombs can appear anywhere in the multiverse, and discovering one should be a campaign-defining event. When running Acererak's dungeons, lean into the puzzle-box nature of the design — every room should feel like it was built by a genius who despises the people trying to navigate it. If Acererak appears in person, he should feel genuinely terrifying: dismissive of the party, amused by their efforts, and capable of ending the encounter at any moment he chooses.

title: Vecna
summary: The Whispered One, Vecna ascended from lich to god of secrets and necromancy, and his severed Hand and Eye are among D&D's most feared artifacts.
content:
## Vecna

**Title/Role:** The Whispered One, The Undying King, God of Secrets and Necromancy
**Setting:** Greyhawk (originally), Multiverse
**Race/Species:** Human (Ascended Lich/Deity)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil

### Overview

Vecna is one of the most powerful and iconic figures in all of D&D — a mortal wizard who became a lich, who became a demigod, who became a god. Born in the Flan city of Fleeth on Oerth, Vecna rose to become the most powerful wizard of his age, conquering a vast empire through necromancy and terror. His lieutenant, the vampire Kas the Bloody-Handed, eventually betrayed him with the very sword Vecna had forged for him — the Sword of Kas. In the ensuing battle, Kas severed Vecna's left hand and gouged out his left eye before being destroyed himself. Vecna was seemingly annihilated, but his essence endured.

The Hand of Vecna and the Eye of Vecna became two of the most infamous artifacts in D&D history. Each grants enormous power to anyone willing to sever their own hand or gouge out their own eye to attach Vecna's remains — but each also slowly corrupts the wielder, bending them toward Vecna's will. These artifacts have been the centerpiece of countless campaigns and have destroyed as many characters through corruption as through combat.

Vecna's enduring theme is the power of secrets. He hoards forbidden knowledge, and his followers — the Cult of the Whispered One — believe that every secret is a source of power. Vecna represents the terrifying idea that knowledge itself can be evil, that some truths are so dangerous that knowing them changes you irrevocably.

### Notable Appearances

- **Eldritch Wizardry (1976)** — The Hand and Eye of Vecna first appeared as artifacts in this OD&D supplement.
- **Vecna Lives! (1990)** — The adventure where Vecna first appeared as a direct antagonist, ascending to demigod status.
- **Vecna Reborn (1998)** — Set in Ravenloft, Vecna schemes to escape the Domains of Dread.
- **Die Vecna Die! (2000)** — The epic adventure bridging 2nd and 3rd Edition, where Vecna attempts to rewrite reality itself. Often cited as one of the most ambitious adventures ever published.
- **Vecna: Eve of Ruin (2024)** — The 5th Edition capstone adventure featuring Vecna as the ultimate villain attempting to reshape the multiverse.
- **Critical Role** — Vecna served as the final villain of Campaign 1, dramatically raising his profile among modern D&D fans.

### Using in Your Campaign

Vecna works on multiple levels. At lower levels, his cult provides secretive, insidious villains who collect forbidden knowledge. At mid levels, the Hand and Eye can appear as temptations — artifacts of immense power with terrible costs. At high levels, Vecna himself can serve as an apocalyptic threat seeking to rewrite reality. The key to Vecna is secrecy: his followers whisper, his plans unfold in shadows, and the party should always feel like they are one step behind, uncovering layers of a conspiracy that goes deeper than they imagined.

title: Tiamat (The Dragon Queen)
summary: The five-headed Queen of Evil Dragons, Tiamat is a scheming goddess-tyrant imprisoned in Avernus who endlessly plots her return to the Material Plane.
content:
## Tiamat (The Dragon Queen)

**Title/Role:** Queen of Evil Dragons, Queen of Chromatic Dragons
**Setting:** Multiverse (Forgotten Realms primarily)
**Race/Species:** Dragon (Deity/Archfiend)
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Tiamat is the five-headed dragon goddess of greed, tyranny, and chromatic dragons. Each of her five heads corresponds to one of the five chromatic dragon types — white, black, green, blue, and red — and each possesses the full intelligence and breath weapon of an ancient dragon of that color. She is one of the most visually striking and immediately recognizable villains in all of D&D, a creature of such overwhelming power that her mere presence reshapes reality around her.

In most D&D cosmologies, Tiamat is imprisoned in Avernus, the first layer of the Nine Hells, where she serves as a reluctant guardian and conspirator alongside (and against) Asmodeus. She is not there by choice — she was banished from the Material Plane and endlessly schemes to return. Her cults on the Material Plane work tirelessly to gather the wealth and perform the rituals necessary to free her, and when they succeed — even partially — the results are catastrophic.

Tiamat is more than just a big monster. She is a political operator, a manipulator, and a tyrant. She commands the loyalty of chromatic dragons through a combination of divine authority, fear, and genuine power. She plays long games spanning centuries, using mortal pawns, dragon cultists, and corrupted rulers to advance her agenda. Her enmity with Bahamut, the Platinum Dragon and god of good dragons, is one of the defining conflicts of D&D cosmology.

### Notable Appearances

- **Original D&D (1975)** — Tiamat appeared in the Greyhawk supplement as the "Dragon Queen" and "Chromatic Dragon."
- **Hoard of the Dragon Queen / Rise of Tiamat (2014)** — The first 5th Edition storyline, in which the Cult of the Dragon attempts to summon Tiamat from Avernus to the Forgotten Realms. The climax can feature a direct confrontation with Tiamat herself.
- **Tyranny of Dragons (combined edition, 2023)** — The revised and combined version of the two-part adventure.
- **Fizban's Treasury of Dragons (2021)** — Expanded lore on Tiamat and Bahamut, including their cosmic origins and the First World.
- **Dungeons & Dragons Cartoon (1983-1985)** — Tiamat appeared as a recurring antagonist in the animated series.

### Using in Your Campaign

Tiamat is the ultimate dragon villain and works best as the final threat of a long campaign. Her cult, the Cult of the Dragon, provides villains at every tier of play — from low-level cultists and dragonclaws to high-level wyrmspeakers and adult dragons. Build toward Tiamat slowly: let the party face her servants, witness the devastation caused by chromatic dragons under her influence, and understand the stakes before the final confrontation. If Tiamat actually appears, it should feel like an apocalyptic event — the sky darkens, the ground shakes, and five heads turn their ancient, malevolent gaze upon the party.

title: Demogorgon
summary: The two-headed Prince of Demons, Demogorgon is the most powerful demon lord of the Abyss, an entity of pure destructive madness.
content:
## Demogorgon

**Title/Role:** Prince of Demons, Lord of the 88th Layer of the Abyss
**Setting:** Multiverse (The Abyss)
**Race/Species:** Demon (Demon Lord)
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil

### Overview

Demogorgon is the self-proclaimed Prince of Demons, and few in the Abyss dare dispute the claim. He is an eighteen-foot-tall monstrosity with two baboon heads atop a reptilian body, his twin tails ending in fanged maws, his arms tentacles of terrible strength. He rules the 88th layer of the Abyss, the Gaping Maw, a primordial wilderness of jungles and oceans teeming with savage life. His dual nature is his defining characteristic — his two heads, Aameul and Hethradiah, each possess independent personalities that scheme against each other even as they cooperate to destroy everything else.

Demogorgon embodies the madness at the heart of the Abyss. His mere presence drives mortal minds to insanity, and his cults on the Material Plane are invariably deranged, practicing blood sacrifice and ritualistic madness. He is not a planner or a schemer in the way that Asmodeus or Vecna are — he is a force of nature, an avatar of primal destruction that seeks dominion through overwhelming power and the sowing of chaos.

The rivalry between Demogorgon and Orcus is one of the great conflicts of the Lower Planes. Both claim the title of Prince of Demons, and their war has raged across the Abyss for millennia, dragging countless other demon lords into shifting alliances and betrayals. This conflict has spilled onto the Material Plane many times, with catastrophic consequences for mortals caught in the crossfire.

### Notable Appearances

- **Eldritch Wizardry (1976)** — Demogorgon first appeared in this OD&D supplement as the most powerful demon.
- **Out of the Abyss (2015)** — The 5th Edition adventure where multiple demon lords, including Demogorgon, are unleashed into the Underdark. Demogorgon serves as the ultimate threat, rampaging through the subterranean world.
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — Detailed lore and a CR 26 stat block.
- **Stranger Things (2016)** — The TV show named its monster "the Demogorgon" after the D&D creature, dramatically raising awareness of the name in popular culture.
- **Savage Tide Adventure Path (2006-2007)** — Dungeon Magazine's epic campaign culminating in a confrontation with Demogorgon.

### Using in Your Campaign

Demogorgon is an apocalyptic-level threat suitable for the climax of a high-level campaign. His cultists — the kuo-toa, certain troglodyte tribes, and deranged surface-dwellers — can provide villains at lower levels. The key to Demogorgon is madness: his influence should be felt through spreading insanity, mutations, and the breakdown of rational order. Characters investigating his cult should feel reality fraying around them. If Demogorgon appears in person, it should be a moment of cosmic horror — this is not a creature that can be reasoned with or bargained with, only survived.

title: Orcus
summary: The Demon Prince of Undeath, Orcus wields the dreaded Wand of Orcus and commands legions of undead from his Abyssal realm of Thanatos.
content:
## Orcus

**Title/Role:** Demon Prince of Undeath, Lord of Thanatos
**Setting:** Multiverse (The Abyss)
**Race/Species:** Demon (Demon Lord)
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil

### Overview

Orcus is the Demon Prince of Undeath, a bloated, goat-headed fiend who rules the Abyssal layer of Thanatos, a realm of darkness and death. He is one of the most ancient and powerful demon lords, and his obsession is singular: the mastery and proliferation of undeath. Where other demon lords seek conquest or destruction for its own sake, Orcus dreams of a multiverse where all life has been extinguished and replaced with undead servitors under his absolute control.

The Wand of Orcus is one of D&D's most iconic artifacts — a skull-topped rod of obsidian that can slay any living creature with a touch and animate the dead in vast numbers. Orcus guards the wand jealously, and its appearance on the Material Plane is always a sign that Orcus's attention has turned in a terrible direction. The wand has been the object of quests, the catalyst for wars, and the doom of countless heroes.

Orcus is distinguished from other demon lords by his patience and intelligence. While he is certainly capable of mindless rampages, he more often works through cults, corrupted clergy, and undead armies that grow slowly and secretly until they are unstoppable. His rivalry with Demogorgon for the title of Prince of Demons is legendary, and he also wages an eternal war against the Raven Queen for dominion over the dead.

### Notable Appearances

- **Eldritch Wizardry (1976)** — Orcus appeared alongside Demogorgon as one of the original demon lords.
- **H1-H4: Bloodstone Adventures (1985-1988)** — An epic AD&D adventure series culminating in a journey to the Abyss to confront Orcus himself.
- **Dead Gods (1997)** — A Planescape adventure dealing with the apparent death and resurrection of Orcus (as Tenebrous).
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — Updated lore and a CR 26 stat block for 5th Edition.
- **The Book of Vile Darkness (2002)** — Extensive lore on Orcus's cults and methods.

### Using in Your Campaign

Orcus is ideal as the power behind a rising tide of undeath. Start small — unexplained undead appearing, graveyards disturbed, a rural cult performing dark rites — and escalate as the campaign progresses. Orcus's cult should feel like a creeping plague: by the time the party realizes the scope of the threat, entire regions may already be compromised. The Wand of Orcus makes an excellent quest object, either to prevent it from reaching the Material Plane or to destroy it. At the highest levels, a direct confrontation with Orcus in Thanatos is one of the most challenging and memorable encounters in D&D.

title: Lolth (The Spider Queen)
summary: The Spider Queen and goddess of the drow, Lolth is a scheming, sadistic deity who rules through fear, treachery, and the absolute subjugation of her followers.
content:
## Lolth (The Spider Queen)

**Title/Role:** The Spider Queen, Queen of the Demonweb Pits, Goddess of the Drow
**Setting:** Multiverse (Greyhawk/Forgotten Realms)
**Race/Species:** Deity (Demon Lord/Goddess)
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil

### Overview

Lolth, the Spider Queen, is the primary deity of the drow and one of the most actively malevolent forces in the D&D multiverse. She dwells in the Demonweb Pits, a layer of the Abyss shaped like an infinite spider's web of passages and portals, from which she manipulates her followers through a combination of divine power, cruel caprice, and absolute terror. Lolth demands that drow society mirror her own nature — treacherous, matriarchal, and utterly ruthless.

Lolth's origin varies by setting, but the most common version holds that she was once Araushnee, an elven goddess of destiny and artistry, consort of the chief elven god Corellon Larethian. Her betrayal of the elven pantheon led to her banishment and transformation into a demon lord, and she dragged the dark elves down with her into the Underdark. Her corruption of the drow is her greatest triumph and her most enduring crime — she has shaped an entire civilization in her own image of cruelty and paranoia.

What makes Lolth uniquely terrifying among D&D villains is the totality of her control over drow society. She is not a distant threat or a lurking menace — she is the constant, suffocating presence that defines every aspect of drow life. Her priestesses rule through her favor, which can be withdrawn at any moment for any reason. Drow who displease her are transformed into driders — horrific spider-elf hybrids — as a warning to others. She encourages backstabbing, assassination, and betrayal among her followers because it keeps them strong and ensures that only the most ruthless rise to power.

### Notable Appearances

- **Q1: Queen of the Demonweb Pits (1980)** — The climax of the famous GDQ series of modules, where the party ventures into Lolth's Abyssal domain.
- **War of the Spider Queen (2002-2005)** — A six-novel series chronicling Lolth's mysterious silence and the chaos it causes in drow society.
- **Out of the Abyss (2015)** — Lolth's influence pervades the Underdark as demon lords rampage through the subterranean world.
- **R.A. Salvatore's Drizzt novels** — Lolth is the ever-present antagonist of drow society, driving the conflict that shapes Drizzt's story.
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — Detailed lore on the elf-drow schism and Lolth's role in it.

### Using in Your Campaign

Lolth is best used as the malevolent force behind drow antagonists rather than as a direct combatant. Her priestesses, her driders, and the politics of drow cities like Menzoberranzan provide antagonists across all levels of play. The most effective way to use Lolth is to show the damage she does to her own followers — drow NPCs who live in constant terror, who betray allies because they must, who have never known trust or kindness. If a drow character in the party has rejected Lolth, she becomes an intensely personal villain. For a truly ambitious campaign, Lolth's silence — a period where she withdraws her favor and drow society collapses into chaos — provides a fascinating and unusual story arc.

title: Asmodeus
summary: The Lord of the Nine Hells and supreme ruler of all devils, Asmodeus is the ultimate schemer whose plans span millennia and whose contracts are inescapable.
content:
## Asmodeus

**Title/Role:** Lord of the Nine Hells, King of Devils, The Lord of Nessus
**Setting:** Multiverse
**Race/Species:** Devil (Greater Deity/Archfiend)
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Asmodeus is the supreme ruler of the Nine Hells, the most powerful devil in existence, and arguably the most dangerous being in the D&D multiverse. He sits upon his throne in Nessus, the deepest and most terrible of the Nine Hells, and from there he orchestrates schemes so vast and intricate that even the gods cannot fully comprehend their scope. Every contract signed with a devil, every soul that falls to the Nine Hells, every temptation that draws a mortal toward evil — all of these ultimately serve Asmodeus.

Asmodeus's origins are deliberately mysterious. Some accounts say he was once the greatest of celestial beings, who argued that evil must be allowed to exist so that good could have meaning — and that this argument was so persuasive that the gods themselves granted him dominion over the plane of punishment. Others say he is a primordial force as old as the multiverse itself. What is certain is that his power is immeasurable, his intelligence is without peer, and his patience is infinite.

What makes Asmodeus unique among D&D villains is that he operates within rules. He does not break contracts — he writes them so carefully that they always favor him. He does not wage open war on the gods — he corrupts their followers through temptation and despair. He represents the terrifying idea that evil can be rational, orderly, and even polite, and that the most dangerous enemy is one who destroys you using your own weaknesses and desires.

### Notable Appearances

- **Monster Manual (1977)** — Asmodeus first appeared as the ruler of the Nine Hells in the original AD&D Monster Manual.
- **Book of Vile Darkness (2002)** — Expanded lore on Asmodeus and the hierarchy of the Nine Hells.
- **Fiendish Codex II: Tyrants of the Nine Hells (2006)** — The definitive sourcebook on the Nine Hells and Asmodeus's rule.
- **4th Edition D&D** — Asmodeus was elevated to full deity status, having consumed the divine essence of the god he slew.
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — Updated lore on the Blood War and Asmodeus's role in the cosmic order.
- **Baldur's Gate: Descent into Avernus (2019)** — While Asmodeus does not appear directly, his influence pervades the adventure set in the first layer of his domain.

### Using in Your Campaign

Asmodeus should never be a direct combatant except in the most epic of campaigns — he is simply too powerful. Instead, use him as the ultimate mastermind whose influence is felt through layers of intermediaries: tiefling warlocks, devil cultists, corrupt nobles who have signed infernal contracts, and archdevils carrying out his will. The most effective way to use Asmodeus is through temptation — offer the party exactly what they want, at a price that seems reasonable, in a contract that seems fair. Then, much later, reveal the hidden cost. Asmodeus represents the corruption of law and order, and his most terrifying quality is that he is always, always right about the letter of the agreement.

title: Xanathar
summary: A paranoid beholder crime lord who rules Waterdeep's underworld from a sprawling underground lair, obsessively protective of his pet goldfish Sylgar.
content:
## Xanathar

**Title/Role:** Beholder Crime Lord of Waterdeep, Leader of the Xanathar Thieves' Guild
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms (Waterdeep)
**Race/Species:** Beholder
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

The Xanathar is the title held by the beholder crime lord who controls the most powerful thieves' guild in Waterdeep, the City of Splendors. The current Xanathar — for there have been several, each a beholder who seized control of the guild — is a paranoid, megalomaniacal tyrant who runs a vast criminal empire from his lair beneath Waterdeep. He controls smuggling, extortion, assassination, and theft operations throughout the city, and his network of informants and enforcers reaches into every level of Waterdhavian society.

What elevates the Xanathar from a standard villain to an iconic character is his personality. He is deeply, hilariously paranoid — convinced that everyone is plotting against him (which, to be fair, they usually are). He is also obsessively devoted to his pet goldfish, Sylgar, whom he considers the most important being in the multiverse. The Xanathar's henchmen live in constant terror that something might happen to Sylgar, because the Xanathar's rage when his fish is threatened is truly terrifying. When a goldfish dies, his minions secretly replace it with an identical one, a deception that has been ongoing for years.

Beneath the dark comedy, the Xanathar is a genuinely dangerous villain. Beholders are among D&D's most iconic and powerful monsters, and one with a criminal empire, a fortified lair, and an army of minions is a formidable threat. His antimagic eye cone makes him nearly impossible to defeat with spellcasting alone, and his paranoia means his lair is trapped, warded, and monitored to an extraordinary degree.

### Notable Appearances

- **Waterdeep and the North (1987)** — Early references to the Xanathar Thieves' Guild in Forgotten Realms lore.
- **Xanathar's Guide to Everything (2017)** — The 5th Edition rules expansion framed as the Xanathar's personal notes, complete with his paranoid commentary and observations about Sylgar. One of the best-selling 5e books.
- **Waterdeep: Dragon Heist (2018)** — The Xanathar is one of four possible main villains in this urban adventure, with a fully detailed lair and organization.
- **Waterdeep: Dungeon of the Mad Mage (2018)** — The Xanathar's influence extends into the upper levels of Undermountain.

### Using in Your Campaign

The Xanathar is perfect for urban campaigns and heist adventures. His criminal network provides a ladder of escalating antagonists, from street-level thugs to mid-level enforcers to the Xanathar himself. Play up his paranoia and his love for Sylgar — these traits make him memorable and even sympathetic in a twisted way, while his beholder nature keeps him terrifying. A great campaign hook is to have the party inadvertently threaten Sylgar or attract the Xanathar's paranoid attention, forcing them into a deadly game of cat-and-mouse through Waterdeep's underworld.

title: Halaster Blackcloak
summary: The Mad Mage of Undermountain, Halaster is an ancient archmage whose sanity shattered centuries ago, leaving him to endlessly reshape the greatest dungeon in the Forgotten Realms.
content:
## Halaster Blackcloak

**Title/Role:** The Mad Mage of Undermountain
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms (Waterdeep)
**Race/Species:** Human (Magically Extended Lifespan)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil

### Overview

Halaster Blackcloak is the creator and eternal inhabitant of Undermountain, the largest and most famous dungeon in the Forgotten Realms. Originally a powerful wizard named Hilather, he came to the location beneath what would become Waterdeep over a thousand years ago, drawn by the magical energy of the Sargauth Level and the ancient dwarven excavations he found there. He drove out the drow, established his lair, and began expanding the dungeon to suit his experiments and whims. Over the centuries, the dungeon consumed him — or perhaps he consumed the dungeon — and his sanity crumbled into brilliant, dangerous madness.

Halaster is not insane in a simple or predictable way. His madness is the madness of a genius mind that has spent too long staring into the fundamental nature of reality. He is by turns lucid and raving, brilliant and nonsensical, helpful and homicidal. He reshapes Undermountain constantly, adding new levels, importing monsters from across the planes, creating elaborate traps and puzzles, and watching adventurers struggle through his creation with a mixture of amusement, contempt, and something almost like affection.

Undermountain itself is Halaster's greatest creation and his prison. The dungeon extends at least 23 levels deep beneath Waterdeep, plus numerous sub-levels and extra-dimensional spaces. It is connected to the Underdark, to other planes, and to Halaster's mad imagination. Entire ecosystems, civilizations, and pocket dimensions exist within its depths, and Halaster is aware of all of them.

### Notable Appearances

- **Ruins of Undermountain (1991)** — The original boxed set detailing Undermountain for 2nd Edition AD&D.
- **Ruins of Undermountain II: The Deep Levels (1994)** — Expanded the dungeon further.
- **Expedition to Undermountain (2007)** — A 3.5 Edition adventure returning to the dungeon.
- **Waterdeep: Dungeon of the Mad Mage (2018)** — The definitive 5th Edition treatment of Undermountain, detailing all 23 levels. Halaster serves as the final boss and the dungeon's omnipresent, unpredictable master.
- **Undermountain: Stardock (2019)** — Additional content set in the dungeon.

### Using in Your Campaign

Halaster is the ultimate dungeon master within the fiction — he literally controls the dungeon. Use him as an unpredictable presence who sometimes helps the party (when it amuses him), sometimes hinders them (when it amuses him), and sometimes appears merely to deliver cryptic warnings or bizarre non sequiturs. The key to Halaster is that he should never be fully predictable. He might teleport a party member to a random level for fun, or he might provide a crucial piece of information in exchange for something absurd. Running Halaster is an invitation to be creative, weird, and occasionally terrifying.

title: Manshoon
summary: The founder of the Zhentarim and a master of contingency magic, Manshoon created so many clones of himself that multiple versions now scheme independently across Faerun.
content:
## Manshoon

**Title/Role:** Founder of the Zhentarim (Black Network)
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Human (Clone-Duplicated)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Manshoon is one of the Forgotten Realms' most enduring villains — a ruthless archmage who founded the Zhentarim, also known as the Black Network, as a vehicle for his ambition to control all trade, politics, and magical power across Faerun. He is a master of contingency magic, specializing in spells that trigger automatically under specific conditions, and he has used this expertise to make himself nearly impossible to permanently destroy.

Manshoon's most distinctive feature is his clones. Over his long career, he created numerous magical clones of himself as insurance against death. When the original Manshoon was destroyed, multiple clones activated simultaneously, each believing itself to be the true Manshoon. This triggered the Manshoon Wars — a secret conflict between several versions of the same wizard, each attempting to eliminate the others and claim the Zhentarim for itself. The result is that multiple Manshoons now exist in the Forgotten Realms, each with slightly different memories, alliances, and plans, but all sharing the same ruthless intelligence and hunger for power.

Manshoon represents a particular kind of villain — the political operator who builds institutions of power. The Zhentarim under his leadership was not just a criminal organization but a parallel government, controlling trade routes, installing puppet rulers, and maintaining armies. Even after Manshoon lost control of the organization, the infrastructure he built endures, and various Manshoon clones continue to scheme to reclaim what they built.

### Notable Appearances

- **FR1: Waterdeep and the North (1987)** — Early appearances of Manshoon and the Zhentarim.
- **The Manshoon Wars** — Referenced throughout Forgotten Realms novels and sourcebooks as a pivotal event in the Black Network's history.
- **Waterdeep: Dragon Heist (2018)** — One Manshoon clone serves as one of four possible main villains in this 5th Edition adventure, operating from a hidden base in Waterdeep.
- **Ed Greenwood's novels** — Manshoon appears throughout the Forgotten Realms novel line, particularly in books dealing with Zhentarim politics.

### Using in Your Campaign

Manshoon is perfect for political intrigue campaigns. His clone situation provides a unique twist — the party might defeat one Manshoon only to discover another operating elsewhere, possibly with conflicting goals. The Zhentarim provides a tiered criminal/political organization for the party to dismantle or infiltrate. Manshoon's contingency magic makes him a challenging opponent who always has an escape plan. Consider introducing multiple Manshoon clones as competing villains whose conflict creates opportunities and dangers for the party.

title: Szass Tam
summary: The lich who rules the magocracy of Thay with an iron fist, Szass Tam commands the Red Wizards and schemes to achieve godhood through mass necromancy.
content:
## Szass Tam

**Title/Role:** Regent of Thay, Zulkir of Necromancy
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms (Thay)
**Race/Species:** Human (Lich)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard (Necromancer)
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil

### Overview

Szass Tam is the undead tyrant who rules Thay, a nation of wizards in eastern Faerun that is one of the most feared powers in the Forgotten Realms. Once one of eight Zulkirs (archmage rulers, each specializing in one school of magic), Szass Tam orchestrated a brutal civil war to seize sole control of Thay, transforming the nation from a fractious magocracy into a necromantic dictatorship. Under his rule, Thay has become a land of the dead — its population increasingly supplemented (and replaced) by undead laborers, soldiers, and servants.

Szass Tam is ancient even by lich standards, having achieved undeath centuries ago through mastery of necromantic magic that few can comprehend. His phylactery is hidden with extraordinary care, and his magical power is sufficient to challenge even the most powerful archmages in the Realms. He is patient, calculating, and utterly ruthless — willing to sacrifice millions of lives (including those of his own citizens) in pursuit of his ultimate goals.

Thay under Szass Tam serves as one of the Forgotten Realms' great geopolitical threats. The Red Wizards of Thay — once independent operators who spread across Faerun establishing enclaves and trade — are now largely under Szass Tam's direct control, serving as spies, agents, and advance forces for his schemes. His ambition extends beyond mere territorial conquest; he seeks to perform rituals of such scale that they would grant him divine power over death itself.

### Notable Appearances

- **Forgotten Realms Campaign Setting (1987 onwards)** — Szass Tam has been a major figure in Realms lore since the setting's inception.
- **The Haunted Lands trilogy (2005-2008)** — Richard Lee Byers' novels chronicling Szass Tam's civil war and rise to sole power in Thay.
- **The Brotherhood of the Griffon series (2009-2013)** — Continued the story of Thay under Szass Tam's rule.
- **Dead in Thay (2014)** — An adventure set in Szass Tam's Doomvault, a massive underground complex used for horrific magical experiments.
- **Tales from the Yawning Portal (2017)** — Includes a version of Dead in Thay.

### Using in Your Campaign

Szass Tam and Thay provide an excellent nation-state villain for campaigns set in or near eastern Faerun. Red Wizards make compelling recurring antagonists at any level — low-level agents, mid-level envoys, and high-level Zulkirs. The horror of Thay itself — a nation where the dead outnumber the living and serve as slave labor — provides powerful narrative material. Szass Tam works best as a distant but ever-present threat whose schemes gradually encroach on the party's sphere of concern, culminating in a high-level confrontation with a lich of extraordinary power.

title: Iggwilv / Tasha
summary: The Witch Queen of Perrenland, known both as the demonologist Iggwilv and as Tasha of Tasha's Cauldron fame — one of D&D's most complex and morally ambiguous figures.
content:
## Iggwilv / Tasha

**Title/Role:** The Witch Queen, Author of the Demonomicon, Tasha of Tasha's Hideous Laughter
**Setting:** Greyhawk (originally), Multiverse
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard/Warlock
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil (historically) / Chaotic Neutral (more recently)

### Overview

Few figures in D&D history are as multifaceted as the woman known by many names: Natasha the Dark, Tasha, Iggwilv, the Witch Queen of Perrenland. She is simultaneously the adoptive daughter of the legendary hag Baba Yaga, the greatest demonologist the multiverse has ever known, the author of the Demonomicon (the definitive text on demon-binding), and the creator of the spell Tasha's Hideous Laughter — one of the most recognizable spell names in all of D&D.

Iggwilv's story is one of boundless ambition and the refusal to be constrained by any moral boundary. Raised by Baba Yaga in the Dancing Hut, she learned magic of extraordinary breadth and power. She seduced and imprisoned the demon lord Graz'zt, extracting his knowledge of the Abyss to write the Demonomicon. She conquered the nation of Perrenland and ruled it as the Witch Queen. She has been killed, resurrected, and reinvented herself multiple times across the centuries.

What makes Iggwilv/Tasha fascinating is her moral complexity. She is unquestionably one of the most dangerous individuals in the multiverse — a woman who has bound demon lords to her will and toppled kingdoms. Yet she is also brilliant, creative, and capable of genuine (if twisted) affection. Her relationship with Graz'zt is simultaneously a tale of exploitation and something resembling dark romance. In recent portrayals, particularly in Tasha's Cauldron of Everything, she has been depicted as more neutral than evil — a figure who has moved beyond simple villainy into something more complex.

### Notable Appearances

- **The Lost Caverns of Tsojcanth (1982)** — Gary Gygax's adventure featuring Iggwilv's lost lair and treasure.
- **The Demonomicon of Iggwilv** — A legendary in-universe text that became a real-world Dragon Magazine column detailing demon lords.
- **Dungeon Magazine #149-150 (2007)** — "Enemies of My Enemy" and "Prince of Demons" featured Iggwilv prominently.
- **Tasha's Cauldron of Everything (2020)** — The major 5th Edition rules expansion framed as Tasha's personal spellbook and notes. Officially connected Tasha and Iggwilv as the same person in 5e canon.
- **The Wild Beyond the Witchlight (2021)** — Tasha/Iggwilv appears as a complex figure connected to the Feywild and the hags of Prismeer.

### Using in Your Campaign

Iggwilv/Tasha is ideal for campaigns that explore moral ambiguity. She can be a villain, a reluctant ally, a source of forbidden knowledge, or all three simultaneously. Her expertise in demonology makes her an invaluable (and dangerous) contact for parties dealing with demonic threats. The Demonomicon can serve as a powerful quest object. Her connection to Baba Yaga and the Dancing Hut opens doors to fairy-tale themed adventures. The key to running Tasha is that she always has her own agenda — she may help the party, but never selflessly, and her price is always steep.

title: Lord Soth
summary: The most infamous death knight in D&D, Lord Soth was a fallen Knight of Solamnia whose pride and inaction doomed the world of Krynn to the Cataclysm.
content:
## Lord Soth

**Title/Role:** Death Knight, Former Knight of the Rose
**Setting:** Dragonlance (Krynn), Ravenloft
**Race/Species:** Human (Death Knight)
**Class (if applicable):** Paladin (Fallen) / Death Knight
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Lord Soth, the Knight of the Black Rose, is the most famous death knight in Dungeons & Dragons and one of the most tragic villains in the game's history. Once Loren Soth, a Knight of the Rose — the highest rank in the Knights of Solamnia — he was a paragon of honor and courage. But his pride, his lust, and ultimately his deliberate inaction led to the Cataclysm that devastated the world of Krynn, killing millions and reshaping continents.

Soth's fall began with an affair with an elf maiden, Isolde, which led to the murder of his first wife. The gods offered him a chance at redemption — a quest that, if completed, would have prevented the Cataclysm. Soth set out with noble intent, but his former companions (themselves corrupted) whispered lies about Isolde's faithfulness. Consumed by jealous rage, Soth turned back. The Cataclysm struck, and as his keep burned around him, Isolde cursed him with her dying breath. He rose as a death knight — an undead warrior of terrible power, condemned to eternal unlife, haunted by the ghostly elven maidens who sing the tale of his betrayal every night.

What makes Lord Soth compelling is that his damnation was entirely his own fault. He was not corrupted by an external force or tricked by a demon. He was given every chance to do the right thing and chose not to, because his pride was stronger than his honor. He is a cautionary tale about the difference between the appearance of virtue and virtue itself.

### Notable Appearances

- **Dragons of Spring Dawning (1985)** — Soth's first major appearance in the Dragonlance novel series, serving Takhisis (the Dragonlance equivalent of Tiamat) as a death knight commander.
- **Knight of the Black Rose (1991)** — The novel by James Lowder, which transported Soth to the Ravenloft setting.
- **Ravenloft Campaign Setting** — Soth served as a Darklord in the Ravenloft setting for several years, ruling the domain of Sithicus.
- **Dragonlance Classics (various)** — Soth appears throughout Dragonlance fiction and game products as a recurring antagonist.
- **Spectre of the Black Rose (1999)** — Continued Soth's story in Ravenloft.

### Using in Your Campaign

Lord Soth is a villain who should evoke a sense of dread and tragedy in equal measure. He is not a cackling madman or a power-hungry schemer — he is a fallen knight who knows exactly what he lost and why, and that knowledge has curdled into bitter rage. Use him as a recurring antagonist whose presence is announced by the mournful singing of ghostly banshees. Encounters with Soth should feel like facing a force of nature — his power word kill, his fireball, and his frightening presence make him lethal in combat, but the real horror is the story behind the armor. Consider giving players fragments of his history so they understand the man he was before the monster he became.

title: Azalin Rex
summary: The lich Darklord of the domain of Darkon in Ravenloft, Azalin is a brilliant wizard-king trapped in an undead existence, forever denied the ability to learn new magic.
content:
## Azalin Rex

**Title/Role:** Darklord of Darkon, The King in the Tower
**Setting:** Ravenloft (Domains of Dread)
**Race/Species:** Human (Lich)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard (Necromancer)
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Azalin Rex is the lich Darklord of Darkon, one of the largest and most powerful domains in the Ravenloft setting. In life, he was Firan Zal'honan, a wizard-king of extraordinary power and ambition who ruled a nation through magical might and political cunning. His transformation into a lich gave him eternal life but cursed him in a way unique among the undead: Azalin cannot learn new magic. He is forever frozen at the level of magical knowledge he possessed at the moment of his transformation, unable to add a single new spell to his repertoire. For a wizard whose identity is built on the pursuit of knowledge, this is an exquisite torture.

Azalin's curse extends beyond his magical limitation. As the Darklord of Darkon, he rules a domain that slowly erases the memories of anyone who enters it, replacing their past with false memories of always having lived in Darkon. Azalin himself is subject to this effect — his own subjects forget that he exists as anything other than a vague, distant ruler. He is a king whom no one truly knows, ruling a nation of people who have forgotten their own identities.

The relationship between Azalin and Strahd von Zarovich is one of the great dynamics of the Ravenloft setting. The two Darklords are reluctant allies, bitter rivals, and occasional co-conspirators who have attempted multiple schemes to escape the Domains of Dread, each plan more elaborate and catastrophic than the last. They despise each other but share a common enemy in the Dark Powers themselves.

### Notable Appearances

- **Ravenloft Campaign Setting (1990, 1994)** — Azalin was introduced as the Darklord of Darkon in the expanded Ravenloft setting.
- **I, Strahd: The War Against Azalin (1998)** — Novel detailing the conflict between Ravenloft's two most prominent Darklords.
- **Azalin (1999)** — A novel exploring Azalin's history and his attempts to escape Ravenloft.
- **Van Richten's Guide to Ravenloft (2021)** — Updated lore for 5th Edition, though Azalin's status is left somewhat ambiguous.
- **Ravenloft: Mist Hunters (2021-2022)** — D&D Adventurers League content featuring Darkon in the wake of Azalin's apparent disappearance.

### Using in Your Campaign

Azalin is a fascinating villain for campaigns set in or connected to Ravenloft. His inability to learn new spells makes him a sympathetic figure in a twisted way — imagine being the most brilliant student in the world, locked in a room with every book you have already read, forever. Use this frustration to drive his plots: Azalin will do anything to break his curse, and his schemes tend to be elaborate, dangerous, and world-threatening. His domain of Darkon provides a unique horror setting where identity itself is under assault. Consider having the party gradually realize that their own memories are being altered the longer they stay in Darkon.

title: Elminster Aumar
summary: The Sage of Shadowdale and Chosen of Mystra, Elminster is the Forgotten Realms' most iconic archmage and one of the most powerful mortal spellcasters in D&D.
content:
## Elminster Aumar

**Title/Role:** Sage of Shadowdale, Chosen of Mystra, Prince of Athalantar
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Human (Chosen of Mystra)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Elminster Aumar is the most famous wizard in the Forgotten Realms and one of the most iconic characters in all of D&D. Over a millennium old, sustained by his bond with the goddess of magic Mystra, Elminster has been a prince, a thief, a priest, a warrior, a woman (for a time, as part of his magical education), and above all a wizard of staggering power. He makes his home in the quiet village of Shadowdale, where he lives in a modest tower, smokes a pipe, dispenses cryptic advice, and occasionally saves the world.

Elminster was created by Ed Greenwood, the creator of the Forgotten Realms, and serves as the setting's narrative anchor — the character through whose eyes the Realms were first explored and described. He is a Chosen of Mystra, one of a handful of mortals who carry a portion of the goddess's divine power, granting him magical abilities that exceed those of normal archmages. He has battled gods, demons, liches, and dragons, and has survived events that have destroyed civilizations.

Despite his immense power, Elminster is characteristically humble, irreverent, and approachable. He prefers to guide and advise rather than directly intervene, believing that heroes must be allowed to find their own paths. He has a fondness for good ale, terrible jokes, and meddling in affairs just enough to nudge events in the right direction. He is also a prolific author and historian, and many of the Forgotten Realms sourcebooks are framed as his writings or dictations.

### Notable Appearances

- **Ed Greenwood's original Dragon Magazine articles (1970s-1980s)** — Elminster first appeared as the narrator and framing device for Greenwood's Forgotten Realms lore articles.
- **Forgotten Realms Campaign Setting (1987 onwards)** — Elminster has been a central figure in every edition of the Realms.
- **The Elminster Series (1999-2012)** — Ed Greenwood's novels chronicling Elminster's long life, from his youth to the modern era.
- **Murder in Baldur's Gate (2013)** — Elminster appears in this multi-edition adventure.
- **Baldur's Gate video game series** — Elminster makes appearances as a helpful NPC in the classic CRPG.

### Using in Your Campaign

Elminster works best as a mentor figure and quest-giver, not as a deus ex machina. His immense power should be balanced by equally immense responsibilities — he is dealing with threats elsewhere, or is magically restrained, or simply believes the party needs to handle this themselves. Use him to deliver exposition, provide crucial but incomplete information, and occasionally demonstrate the scale of power that exists in the world. Resist the temptation to have him solve problems for the party — instead, have him point them in the right direction with a wry comment and a puff of pipe smoke.

title: Drizzt Do'Urden
summary: The drow ranger who rejected the evil of Menzoberranzan to become a hero of the surface world, Drizzt is arguably the most famous character in D&D fiction.
content:
## Drizzt Do'Urden

**Title/Role:** Ranger of the North, Hero of Icewind Dale
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Drow (Dark Elf)
**Class (if applicable):** Ranger/Fighter
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Drizzt Do'Urden is the most famous character in Dungeons & Dragons fiction — a drow elf who rejected the cruelty and evil of his homeland, the underground city of Menzoberranzan, to forge a life of heroism on the surface world. Created by author R.A. Salvatore, Drizzt has been the protagonist of over 40 novels spanning more than three decades, making him one of the most extensively chronicled characters in fantasy literature.

Born into House Do'Urden, a noble house of Menzoberranzan, Drizzt was trained as a warrior at the Academy but could never embrace the treachery, cruelty, and worship of Lolth that defined drow society. After years of internal conflict, he fled Menzoberranzan and spent decades wandering the Underdark alone before finally emerging onto the surface near Icewind Dale. There he found friendship, purpose, and a new family among the Companions of the Hall — the dwarf king Bruenor Battlehammer, the human warrior Catti-brie, the halfling Regis, and the barbarian Wulfgar.

Drizzt is renowned as one of the greatest swordsmen in the Realms, fighting with two scimitars — Twinkle and Icingdeath — in a style of breathtaking speed and precision. He is accompanied by Guenhwyvar, a magical black panther summoned from a figurine of wondrous power. But what defines Drizzt is not his combat prowess — it is his moral compass, his constant struggle against the prejudice he faces as a drow on the surface, and his philosophical reflections on the nature of good, evil, and identity.

### Notable Appearances

- **The Crystal Shard (1988)** — Drizzt's first appearance, though not as protagonist. The first book of the Icewind Dale Trilogy.
- **Homeland (1990)** — The prequel novel detailing Drizzt's birth and upbringing in Menzoberranzan, widely considered one of the best D&D novels ever written.
- **The Legend of Drizzt series (1988-present)** — Over 40 novels following Drizzt's adventures across centuries of Realms history.
- **Baldur's Gate: Dark Alliance (2001)** — Drizzt appears as a special encounter in this action RPG.
- **Drizzt comic series (various)** — Multiple comic adaptations of Salvatore's novels.
- **Magic: The Gathering** — Drizzt has been featured on multiple MTG cards in D&D crossover sets.

### Using in Your Campaign

Drizzt is best used sparingly in a campaign — he is so famous that overuse can overshadow the player characters. A brief encounter where Drizzt passes through the party's area, offers advice or aid in a fight, and moves on can be a memorable moment. His existence in the setting provides important context for drow player characters — Drizzt proved that drow can choose good, but he also demonstrated how difficult that path is. If you use Drizzt, remember that he is a thoughtful, somewhat melancholy figure who carries the weight of constantly proving himself against prejudice — not just a cool fighter with two swords.

title: Mordenkainen
summary: The archmage of Greyhawk and leader of the Circle of Eight, Mordenkainen is a powerful wizard dedicated to maintaining the cosmic Balance between good and evil.
content:
## Mordenkainen

**Title/Role:** Archmage of Greyhawk, Leader of the Circle of Eight
**Setting:** Greyhawk, Multiverse
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** True Neutral (Balance-focused)

### Overview

Mordenkainen is one of the most powerful wizards in D&D and the creator of some of the game's most famous spells — Mordenkainen's Magnificent Mansion, Mordenkainen's Faithful Hound, Mordenkainen's Sword, and many others. Originally the player character of Gary Gygax himself, Mordenkainen has evolved from a personal character into one of the defining figures of D&D lore, an archmage whose influence spans the multiverse.

Mordenkainen is the founder and leader of the Circle of Eight, a cabal of powerful wizards dedicated to maintaining the Balance — the equilibrium between the forces of good and evil, law and chaos, that Mordenkainen believes is essential to the health of the multiverse. This philosophy makes Mordenkainen a deeply unusual figure in fantasy: he will aid good against overwhelming evil, but he will also undermine good if it becomes too dominant, believing that the ascendancy of any single force would be catastrophic for all.

This Balance philosophy has led Mordenkainen to make morally questionable decisions — supporting villains when heroes grow too powerful, withholding aid when intervention would tip the scales too far in one direction, and maintaining relationships with beings that most would consider irredeemably evil. He is respected but not always liked, trusted but not always trustworthy, and his interventions in world affairs are guided by a cosmic calculus that few can fully understand.

### Notable Appearances

- **Gary Gygax's home campaign (1970s)** — Mordenkainen originated as Gygax's personal character in the original Greyhawk campaign.
- **Various Greyhawk modules and sourcebooks** — Mordenkainen and the Circle of Eight are central to Greyhawk's ongoing storylines.
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — The 5th Edition sourcebook framed as Mordenkainen's research into the great conflicts of the multiverse.
- **Curse of Strahd (2016)** — Mordenkainen appears as a mad wizard trapped in Barovia, having failed in an attempt to defeat Strahd.
- **Mordenkainen Presents: Monsters of the Multiverse (2022)** — Another sourcebook bearing his name.
- **Vecna: Eve of Ruin (2024)** — Mordenkainen plays a role in the fight against Vecna's multiverse-threatening scheme.

### Using in Your Campaign

Mordenkainen is a fascinating NPC because the party can never be entirely sure whose side he is on. He might provide crucial aid against a villain, only to be revealed later as having also aided that villain at an earlier point to prevent a different threat from becoming dominant. Use him as a source of powerful magic, deep lore, and uncomfortable moral questions. His Balance philosophy should challenge the party's assumptions about good and evil. His spells are famous enough that characters might seek him out to learn magic, providing an excellent quest hook.

title: Volothamp Geddarm (Volo)
summary: The Forgotten Realms' most famous (and unreliable) author, explorer, and self-promoter, Volo has lent his name to several iconic D&D sourcebooks.
content:
## Volothamp Geddarm (Volo)

**Title/Role:** Author, Explorer, Raconteur, Self-Proclaimed Expert on Everything
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard (low-level)
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Volothamp Geddarm, universally known as Volo, is the Forgotten Realms' most prolific, most famous, and most frequently wrong author. He has written guides to seemingly everything in Faerun — monsters, cities, magic items, taverns — and while his work contains genuine insights and useful information, it is also riddled with exaggerations, fabrications, and outright lies. Volo is part Indiana Jones, part Baron Munchausen, and part used-car salesman: a genuinely curious and adventurous soul who cannot resist embellishing a good story.

Volo is a minor wizard at best — his magical abilities are modest, and he survives his many adventures through a combination of luck, charm, quick wits, and the occasional intervention of more powerful friends (particularly Elminster, who frequently corrects Volo's manuscripts with exasperated annotations). What Volo lacks in power, he makes up for in personality. He is irrepressibly optimistic, endlessly talkative, and genuinely enthusiastic about the wonders of the world, even when those wonders are trying to eat him.

As a literary device, Volo serves an important function in the Forgotten Realms: he is the unreliable narrator through whom the setting's lore is filtered, giving writers and DMs permission to treat published lore as sometimes inaccurate and always open to interpretation. His guides are presented as in-universe publications that characters might actually own and reference, adding a layer of verisimilitude to the setting.

### Notable Appearances

- **Volo's Guide series (1990s)** — A series of 2nd Edition sourcebooks including Volo's Guide to Waterdeep, Volo's Guide to the Sword Coast, Volo's Guide to Cormyr, and many others.
- **Volo's Guide to Monsters (2016)** — The 5th Edition monster supplement framed as Volo's research, complete with Elminster's sarcastic footnotes. One of the best-selling 5e books.
- **Waterdeep: Dragon Heist (2018)** — Volo appears as a key NPC who hires the party and provides them with a tavern as their base of operations.
- **Ed Greenwood's novels** — Volo appears throughout Forgotten Realms fiction as a colorful recurring character.

### Using in Your Campaign

Volo is the perfect quest-giver NPC: enthusiastic, well-connected, frequently in over his head, and always willing to hire adventurers to do the dangerous parts of his research. He can send the party to investigate locations for his next guide, ask them to verify (or debunk) rumors he has heard, or simply drag them into trouble by association. His unreliability makes him useful for DMs who want to provide information to the party without guaranteeing its accuracy. Volo's most effective use is as a recurring comic relief character who is nonetheless genuinely likeable and occasionally provides crucial information — buried under layers of exaggeration.

title: Minsc (and Boo)
summary: The beloved berserker ranger of Rashemen and his miniature giant space hamster Boo, Minsc is one of D&D's most endearing and quotable characters.
content:
## Minsc (and Boo)

**Title/Role:** Ranger of Rashemen, Berserker Warrior
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Ranger
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Minsc is a large, bald, heavily tattooed ranger from the nation of Rashemen in the Forgotten Realms, and he is one of the most beloved characters in D&D history — not despite his simplicity, but because of it. Minsc is not clever, not subtle, and not politically savvy. He is, however, brave beyond measure, loyal to a fault, and possessed of a moral clarity that more sophisticated characters often lack. He knows that evil must be punished, that the innocent must be protected, and that his hamster Boo is a miniature giant space hamster of extraordinary intelligence.

Boo is a hamster. Whether Boo is actually a miniature giant space hamster (a creature from the Spelljammer setting) or simply a normal hamster that Minsc believes to be extraordinary is one of D&D's great unsolved mysteries. What is certain is that Minsc consults Boo regularly, credits Boo with tactical insights, and flies into a berserker rage if anyone threatens his small companion. The relationship between the huge warrior and his tiny hamster is pure comedy gold and genuine emotional resonance in equal measure.

Minsc's fame comes primarily from the Baldur's Gate video game series, where he was a companion character voiced with bombastic enthusiasm. His battle cries — "Go for the eyes, Boo! GO FOR THE EYES!" and "Butt-kicking for goodness!" — have become some of the most quoted lines in gaming history. He represents D&D at its most joyful: a game where a giant warrior and his hamster can save the world through pure-hearted heroism and enthusiastic violence.

### Notable Appearances

- **Baldur's Gate (1998)** — Minsc first appeared as a recruitable companion in BioWare's landmark CRPG. He quickly became the fan-favorite character.
- **Baldur's Gate II: Shadows of Amn (2000)** — Minsc returned as a companion, with expanded dialogue and the introduction of many of his most famous quotes.
- **Baldur's Gate III (2023)** — Minsc returns as a recruitable companion, having been petrified for over a century and recently freed. Boo is still with him.
- **Legends of Baldur's Gate (comics, 2014-2016)** — Minsc stars in IDW's D&D comic series.
- **Idle Champions of the Forgotten Realms** — Minsc appears as a playable champion.
- **Magic: The Gathering** — Minsc and Boo have been featured on MTG cards.

### Using in Your Campaign

Minsc is best used as a loveable ally NPC who provides comic relief and heartfelt moments in equal measure. Play him with complete sincerity — Minsc is not ironic or self-aware; he genuinely believes everything he says. His berserker rages should be both funny and genuinely intimidating. Always include Boo, and always leave it ambiguous whether Boo is actually providing tactical advice or whether Minsc is just interpreting hamster squeaks. Minsc works best in contrast to more cynical or morally complex characters — his straightforward goodness should highlight the murkiness around him.

title: Jarlaxle Baenre
summary: The flamboyant drow mercenary leader of Bregan D'aerthe, Jarlaxle is a charming rogue who plays every side and always comes out on top.
content:
## Jarlaxle Baenre

**Title/Role:** Leader of Bregan D'aerthe, Mercenary Captain
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Drow (Dark Elf)
**Class (if applicable):** Fighter/Rogue
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral

### Overview

Jarlaxle Baenre is the founder and leader of Bregan D'aerthe, an elite mercenary company composed primarily of houseless drow males — outcasts from the matriarchal society of Menzoberranzan. Where Drizzt Do'Urden rejected drow society through moral conviction, Jarlaxle rejected it through sheer pragmatism and style, building his own power base outside the traditional house structure and making himself indispensable to every faction in the Underdark and beyond.

Jarlaxle is one of the most charismatic characters in D&D fiction — flamboyant, witty, endlessly adaptable, and possessed of an almost supernatural ability to land on his feet regardless of circumstances. He wears an enormous plumed hat (which is actually a magical item), carries an arsenal of magical trinkets and weapons, and approaches every situation as an opportunity for profit and entertainment. He has no loyalty except to his own survival and prosperity, but he has a code: he keeps his word (when he gives it explicitly), he protects his people, and he never kills when manipulation will suffice.

What makes Jarlaxle fascinating is his moral ambiguity. He has worked with heroes and villains with equal enthusiasm, played both sides of conflicts simultaneously, and made deals with devils, demons, and archmages without blinking. He is not evil in the traditional sense — he causes no unnecessary suffering and has shown genuine compassion on occasions — but he is utterly amoral when it comes to business. He is the ultimate survivor in a world that tries to kill everyone, and he does it with panache.

### Notable Appearances

- **R.A. Salvatore's Drizzt novels** — Jarlaxle appears throughout the series as a recurring character who shifts between ally and antagonist. The Sellswords trilogy (2002-2004) and Homecoming trilogy (2015-2016) feature him prominently.
- **Waterdeep: Dragon Heist (2018)** — Jarlaxle is one of four possible main villains (though calling him a villain is a stretch — he is more of an opportunist).
- **Timeless / Boundless / Relentless trilogy (2018-2020)** — Salvatore's novels featuring Jarlaxle and his companion Artemis Entreri as co-protagonists alongside Drizzt.
- **Baldur's Gate III (2023)** — Referenced in the game's lore.

### Using in Your Campaign

Jarlaxle is the ultimate wildcard NPC. He can be a patron, an adversary, an ally, or all three in the same adventure. The key to running Jarlaxle is that he always has an angle — every interaction with the party is also an opportunity for Jarlaxle to advance his own interests. He should be charming, generous with information (some of which is misleading), and impossible to fully trust. Use him to complicate the party's moral landscape: he might offer genuinely good help, but for reasons that serve his own ends. Bregan D'aerthe provides an excellent organizational resource — a mercenary company that can appear anywhere and whose loyalties are always negotiable.

title: Bruenor Battlehammer
summary: The gruff but fiercely loyal dwarf king of Mithral Hall and founding member of the Companions of the Hall, Bruenor is the quintessential D&D dwarf.
content:
## Bruenor Battlehammer

**Title/Role:** King of Mithral Hall, Founding Member of the Companions of the Hall
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms
**Race/Species:** Dwarf (Shield Dwarf)
**Class (if applicable):** Fighter
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Bruenor Battlehammer is the epitome of the D&D dwarf — gruff, stubborn, fiercely loyal, fond of ale and battle, and possessed of a heart of gold beneath a crusty exterior. He is the rightful king of Mithral Hall, an ancient dwarven stronghold that he spent decades searching for before finally reclaiming it from the shadow dragon Shimmergloom and the gray dwarves who had infested it. His quest to reclaim Mithral Hall is one of the foundational stories of the Forgotten Realms and the Drizzt novel series.

Bruenor is the adoptive father of Catti-brie, a human woman he raised as his own after finding her as an orphan in the aftermath of a goblin raid. This relationship — the gruff dwarf warrior who raised a human daughter with genuine love and devotion — is one of the most touching in D&D fiction and demonstrates the depth beneath Bruenor's rough exterior. He is also Drizzt Do'Urden's closest friend and the first person on the surface to accept the drow ranger without prejudice, a decision that speaks to Bruenor's ability to judge character over appearance.

As a member of the Companions of the Hall (alongside Drizzt, Catti-brie, Wulfgar, and Regis), Bruenor has participated in some of the most epic adventures in Forgotten Realms history. He has died and been reincarnated, lost and reclaimed his kingdom multiple times, and faced threats ranging from ancient dragons to demon lords. Through it all, his defining characteristics remain: unwavering loyalty, stubborn courage, and a talent for hitting things with an axe.

### Notable Appearances

- **The Crystal Shard (1988)** — Bruenor's first appearance in the Icewind Dale Trilogy.
- **Streams of Silver (1989)** — The novel focused on Bruenor's quest to reclaim Mithral Hall.
- **The Legend of Drizzt series (1988-present)** — Bruenor appears throughout the series as a central character.
- **Baldur's Gate video game series** — Referenced throughout the games' lore.
- **D&D sourcebooks** — Mithral Hall appears in multiple Forgotten Realms sourcebooks.

### Using in Your Campaign

Bruenor represents everything players love about dwarves, and using him (or a character inspired by him) can anchor a campaign in classic D&D themes of loyalty, honor, and the reclamation of lost glories. He works well as a quest-giver (reclaim this ancestral home, defend this dwarven stronghold) or as an ally who fights alongside the party. Play him as gruff and impatient with foolishness, but capable of surprising tenderness — especially toward his adopted family. His defining trait is that he will never abandon his friends, no matter the odds.
