title: Laeral Silverhand
summary: The Open Lord of Waterdeep and one of the Seven Sisters, Laeral is a Chosen of Mystra who governs the City of Splendors with pragmatic wisdom and formidable magical power.
content:
## Laeral Silverhand

**Title/Role:** Open Lord of Waterdeep, Chosen of Mystra, One of the Seven Sisters
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms (Waterdeep)
**Race/Species:** Human (Chosen of Mystra)
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Laeral Silverhand is the current Open Lord of Waterdeep, the publicly known ruler of the greatest city in the Forgotten Realms. She is also one of the Seven Sisters — seven daughters of the goddess Mystra, each a Chosen imbued with a portion of the goddess's divine magical power. Laeral is over 700 years old, sustained by her Chosen status, and she brings centuries of experience in magic, politics, warfare, and diplomacy to her role as Waterdeep's leader.

Laeral's life has been marked by extraordinary highs and devastating lows. She was once corrupted by the Crown of Horns, an artifact of the dead god Myrkul, which drove her mad for years. She was previously married to Khelben "Blackstaff" Aranson, one of the most powerful wizards in the Realms, and his death in the Spellplague era left a wound that has never fully healed. Despite these traumas, she emerged to take the reins of Waterdeep at a time of great instability, bringing order, justice, and a firm but fair hand to the City of Splendors.

As Open Lord, Laeral is a political figure as much as a magical one. She must balance the interests of Waterdeep's noble families, merchant guilds, criminal underworld, and diverse population while also dealing with external threats. She is pragmatic enough to work with morally questionable allies when necessary, and powerful enough to back up her decisions with arcane force when diplomacy fails. Her Seven Sisters heritage gives her a network of equally powerful siblings across the Realms.

### Notable Appearances

- **Forgotten Realms sourcebooks (1990s onwards)** — Laeral has been part of Realms lore since the Seven Sisters were first described.
- **Waterdeep: Dragon Heist (2018)** — Laeral is a major NPC as the Open Lord of Waterdeep, providing quests and political context for the adventure.
- **Waterdeep: Dungeon of the Mad Mage (2018)** — Laeral's influence extends into the politics surrounding Undermountain.
- **The Seven Sisters (1995)** — Ed Greenwood's sourcebook detailing Laeral and her sisters.
- **Sword Coast Adventurer's Guide (2015)** — Describes Laeral's rise to Open Lord.

### Using in Your Campaign

Laeral is an excellent patron NPC for Waterdeep-based campaigns — a powerful ruler who respects adventurers and is willing to employ them for sensitive tasks that official channels cannot handle. She is not a distant or unapproachable figure; she is known for walking the streets of Waterdeep, visiting taverns, and speaking with common citizens. Use her to provide the party with political context, official sanction for their activities, and occasional magical assistance. Her history with the Crown of Horns provides a powerful lesson about the dangers of evil artifacts, and her grief over Khelben adds emotional depth.

title: Fizban / Bahamut
summary: The Platinum Dragon, Bahamut is the god of good dragons who often walks the mortal world disguised as a doddering old man named Fizban, dispensing wisdom through apparent senility.
content:
## Fizban / Bahamut

**Title/Role:** The Platinum Dragon, God of Good Dragons, King of Metallic Dragons
**Setting:** Multiverse (Dragonlance as Fizban, universal as Bahamut)
**Race/Species:** Dragon (Deity)
**Class (if applicable):** N/A
**Alignment:** Lawful Good

### Overview

Bahamut, the Platinum Dragon, is the god of good metallic dragons and one of the most powerful benevolent beings in the D&D multiverse. He stands in eternal opposition to Tiamat, the Queen of Evil Dragons, and this cosmic rivalry between the two dragon deities is one of the foundational conflicts of D&D cosmology. Where Tiamat embodies greed, tyranny, and destruction, Bahamut represents justice, wisdom, and protection.

Bahamut's most famous mortal disguise is Fizban — a befuddled, absent-minded old wizard who appeared in the Dragonlance saga as a seemingly comic figure who turned out to be one of the most powerful beings in the world. As Fizban, Bahamut wandered Krynn during the War of the Lance, aiding the Heroes of the Lance through apparent bumbling that concealed divine wisdom. He would forget spells at critical moments, trip over his robes, and deliver crucial information buried in rambling anecdotes — all while subtly guiding events toward the defeat of Takhisis (Tiamat's Dragonlance aspect).

In his true form, Bahamut is a magnificent platinum dragon of breathtaking size and radiance. He is attended by seven ancient gold dragons who serve as his court and companions. He can also appear as a young monk, a kindly nobleman, or any number of unassuming mortal forms. His philosophy emphasizes that true strength lies in mercy and wisdom, not raw power, and he encourages his followers to protect the weak, oppose tyranny, and seek justice — but always with compassion.

### Notable Appearances

- **Dragonlance Chronicles (1984-1985)** — Fizban is a major character in the original Dragonlance trilogy, providing comic relief and hidden divine guidance.
- **Dragonlance Legends (1986)** — Fizban/Paladine appears in the sequel trilogy.
- **Monster Manual (every edition)** — Bahamut has been detailed in the core D&D rules since the beginning.
- **Fizban's Treasury of Dragons (2021)** — The 5th Edition sourcebook framed as Fizban's notes on dragonkind, blending humor with deep lore about the nature of dragons in D&D cosmology.
- **Rise of Tiamat (2014)** — Bahamut's influence opposes Tiamat's return.

### Using in Your Campaign

Bahamut/Fizban works beautifully as a mysterious mentor figure who appears in humble guise. As Fizban, play him as genuinely amusing — the kind of old man who calls everyone by the wrong name, tells stories that seem irrelevant but contain crucial clues, and occasionally performs accidental feats of remarkable magic. The reveal that this doddering old man is actually a god should be earned through a long buildup. Alternatively, Bahamut can serve as a divine patron for good-aligned campaigns involving dragons, providing quests through his mortal agents and rewarding those who protect the innocent.

title: Bigby
summary: The archmage famous for his iconic hand spells, Bigby is a member of Greyhawk's Circle of Eight and one of D&D's most spell-prolific wizards.
content:
## Bigby

**Title/Role:** Archmage, Member of the Circle of Eight
**Setting:** Greyhawk
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Neutral Good (historically Neutral)

### Overview

Bigby is one of the most recognized names in D&D, not because of his adventuring exploits or political machinations, but because of his spells. Bigby's Hand — along with its variants Bigby's Clenched Fist, Bigby's Grasping Hand, Bigby's Crushing Hand, and others — is one of the most iconic spell families in the game. Every wizard who has conjured a giant spectral hand to crush, grab, or slap an enemy owes a debt to Bigby's magical research.

In Greyhawk lore, Bigby began as an apprentice to the archmage Mordenkainen, eventually rising to become a powerful wizard in his own right and a member of the Circle of Eight. His early history is colorful — he was originally a young villain who served the evil wizard Leomund (in some accounts) before being reformed and taken under Mordenkainen's wing. This redemption arc, from a servant of evil to a champion of balance and eventually good, gives Bigby a narrative depth that many spell-named wizards lack.

Bigby's magical specialty is force magic — the creation and manipulation of pure magical force, shaped into hands, walls, and barriers. His research in this area has been so thorough and influential that virtually every wizard in the multiverse who learns force-shaping magic is, whether they know it or not, building on Bigby's foundational work. He is, in a very real sense, the father of an entire school of magical practice.

### Notable Appearances

- **Greyhawk supplement (1975)** — Bigby's spells first appeared in the original D&D supplements.
- **Greyhawk campaign and sourcebooks** — Bigby appears throughout Greyhawk lore as a member of the Circle of Eight.
- **Player's Handbook (every edition)** — Bigby's Hand spells are core spells in virtually every edition of D&D.
- **Bigby Presents: Glory of the Giants (2023)** — The 5th Edition sourcebook on giants, framed as Bigby's research and narrated by his homunculus companion Diancastra.
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — Referenced as part of the Circle of Eight.

### Using in Your Campaign

Bigby is an excellent NPC for campaigns that involve magical research, the Circle of Eight, or Greyhawk politics. He can serve as a mentor for wizards, a quest-giver seeking rare magical components, or a political figure navigating the complex dynamics of the Circle. His force magic specialty makes him visually distinctive in combat — a wizard whose signature move is conjuring giant glowing hands. His redemption backstory provides an interesting angle for parties dealing with reformed villains or the question of whether evil can truly be overcome.

title: Tenser
summary: The archmage who created Tenser's Floating Disk and Tenser's Transformation, he is a stalwart good-aligned member of Greyhawk's Circle of Eight.
content:
## Tenser

**Title/Role:** Archmage, Member of the Circle of Eight, Lord of the Fortress of Unknown Depths
**Setting:** Greyhawk
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard
**Alignment:** Lawful Good

### Overview

Tenser is an archmage of Greyhawk known for two things: his famous spells and his unwavering commitment to good in a group (the Circle of Eight) that officially espouses neutrality and balance. Where Mordenkainen insists on maintaining equilibrium between good and evil, Tenser has always argued that good should be actively championed and evil actively opposed. This philosophical disagreement has put him at odds with the Circle's leadership on multiple occasions, and he has been expelled from the group more than once for acting on his convictions rather than the Circle's agenda.

Tenser's most famous spells reflect his practical, militant approach to wizardry. Tenser's Floating Disk is a utilitarian workhorse spell beloved by adventurers for carrying treasure and equipment. Tenser's Transformation is far more dramatic — a spell that temporarily transforms a wizard into a powerful warrior, reflecting Tenser's belief that a wizard should be prepared to fight in melee when necessary. This willingness to get his hands dirty, both literally and metaphorically, is characteristic of Tenser's approach to both magic and morality.

Tenser makes his home in the Fortress of Unknown Depths, a castle on the shores of the Nyr Dyv (the Lake of Unknown Depths) in Greyhawk. From this base, he conducts magical research, opposes evil wherever he finds it, and occasionally clashes with his Circle of Eight colleagues over the proper use of their considerable collective power. He has been killed by enemies of the Circle and resurrected by his allies, a cycle that has only strengthened his resolve.

### Notable Appearances

- **Gary Gygax's home campaign (1970s)** — Tenser originated as the character of Gygax's son Ernie (Tenser is an anagram of Ernest).
- **Greyhawk sourcebooks and adventures** — Tenser appears throughout Greyhawk lore as a prominent archmage.
- **Player's Handbook (every edition)** — Tenser's Floating Disk has been a core spell since the earliest editions of D&D.
- **Return of the Eight (1998)** — An adventure dealing with the reformation of the Circle of Eight after a devastating attack.
- **Mordenkainen's Tome of Foes (2018)** — Referenced as part of the Circle of Eight.

### Using in Your Campaign

Tenser is an ideal patron for good-aligned parties — a powerful wizard who actively wants evil defeated, not merely balanced. He can provide quests, magical support, and political connections, and his conflict with Mordenkainen's Balance philosophy provides interesting narrative tension. Tenser's militant attitude makes him a good fit for campaigns with a clear good-versus-evil dynamic. His Fortress of Unknown Depths makes an excellent base of operations or a location to defend against attack. His spells are so ubiquitous that parties are likely already using his magic without knowing the man behind it.

title: Raistlin Majere
summary: The tragic and brilliant wizard of Dragonlance whose quest for power nearly destroyed the world, Raistlin is one of D&D's most complex and compelling characters.
content:
## Raistlin Majere

**Title/Role:** Master of Past and Present, Wielder of the Staff of Magius
**Setting:** Dragonlance (Krynn)
**Race/Species:** Human
**Class (if applicable):** Wizard (Black Robes)
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil (later complex/debatable)

### Overview

Raistlin Majere is one of the most complex and compelling characters in D&D fiction — a frail, brilliant, bitter wizard whose hunger for power led him to challenge the gods themselves. Born a sickly twin alongside his robust brother Caramon, Raistlin grew up overshadowed and resentful, channeling his physical weakness into magical study of unprecedented intensity. His Test at the Tower of High Sorcery left him with golden skin, hourglass-shaped pupils that saw all living things as aging and decaying, and a wracking cough that would plague him forever — the price of magical power.

Raistlin began his career wearing the Red Robes of neutrality but eventually donned the Black Robes of evil magic, seeking the power of the dark goddess Takhisis. His ambition was staggering: he sought not merely to defeat Takhisis, but to replace her — to become a god. In the Dragonlance Legends trilogy, he succeeded in entering the Abyss and confronting Takhisis directly, and the story strongly implies he could have won. But at the last moment, shown a vision of the empty, lifeless world that would result from his victory, he chose to sacrifice himself rather than reign over nothing.

What makes Raistlin endlessly fascinating is the question of whether his story is one of redemption or tragedy. He committed terrible acts — murdered, manipulated, and betrayed those who loved him — but he also saved the world at the cost of his own existence. He genuinely loved his brother Caramon, even as he resented and mistreated him. He was capable of unexpected kindness, as when he protected the young kender Bupu. Raistlin defies easy moral categorization, which is precisely why he remains one of D&D's most discussed characters decades after his creation.

### Notable Appearances

- **Dragons of Autumn Twilight (1984)** — Raistlin's first appearance in the original Dragonlance Chronicles trilogy by Margaret Weis and Tracy Hickman.
- **Dragonlance Legends trilogy (1986)** — The sequel trilogy focusing on Raistlin's quest for godhood and his relationship with Caramon. Widely considered the peak of Dragonlance fiction.
- **The Soulforge (1998)** — Prequel novel by Margaret Weis detailing Raistlin's youth and his Test.
- **Brothers in Arms (1999)** — Continues the prequel story of Raistlin and Caramon.
- **Dragonlance: Shadow of the Dragon Queen (2022)** — The 5th Edition Dragonlance adventure, set during a different era but in the same world Raistlin shaped.

### Using in Your Campaign

Raistlin-inspired characters and storylines work brilliantly in campaigns that explore the cost of power. His archetype — the brilliant but physically fragile wizard who pays a terrible price for every advancement — resonates deeply with players. As an NPC, a Raistlin-like figure provides moral complexity: an ally who is genuinely useful but clearly dangerous, whose help always comes with strings attached. If using Raistlin himself in a Dragonlance campaign, remember that he is not a villain in the traditional sense — he is a tragic figure whose worst enemy is himself.

title: Tasslehoff Burrfoot
summary: The irrepressible kender hero of the Dragonlance saga, Tasslehoff's fearless curiosity and innocent kleptomania made him one of D&D's most beloved characters.
content:
## Tasslehoff Burrfoot

**Title/Role:** Hero of the Lance, Time-Traveling Adventurer
**Setting:** Dragonlance (Krynn)
**Race/Species:** Kender
**Class (if applicable):** Rogue (Handler)
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good

### Overview

Tasslehoff Burrfoot — Tas to his friends, which is everyone he has ever met — is the most famous kender in D&D history and one of the most beloved characters in the Dragonlance saga. Kender are a race unique to the Dragonlance setting: small, curious, utterly fearless (they are literally incapable of feeling fear), and possessed of an innocent habit of picking up interesting objects that happen to belong to other people. They do not steal — they "borrow" and "find" things, and are genuinely baffled when accused of theft.

Tas embodies everything wonderful and maddening about kender. He is relentlessly cheerful, endlessly curious, completely fearless in the face of dragons and gods alike, and constitutionally incapable of keeping his hands to himself. His pouches are always full of fascinating objects that he has "found" — including, occasionally, plot-critical items that he picked up three chapters ago without anyone noticing. He drives his companions to distraction with his constant chatter, his inability to stay out of trouble, and his tendency to wander off at the worst possible moments.

But beneath the comic relief, Tas is genuinely heroic. When the fate of the world hung in the balance, it was Tas who played a pivotal role — his unique kender nature made him resistant to certain divine manipulations, and his willingness to sacrifice himself for his friends proved that courage is not the absence of fear but the choice to act regardless. His death and the events surrounding it are among the most emotionally powerful moments in Dragonlance fiction.

### Notable Appearances

- **Dragons of Autumn Twilight (1984)** — Tas's first appearance as one of the Heroes of the Lance in the original Dragonlance Chronicles.
- **Dragonlance Chronicles trilogy (1984-1985)** — Tas provides comic relief and crucial plot moments throughout the War of the Lance.
- **Dragonlance Legends trilogy (1986)** — Tas accidentally travels through time using a magical device, setting in motion events that reshape the story.
- **War of Souls trilogy (2000-2002)** — Tas's story reaches its emotional climax with themes of sacrifice and fate.
- **Kendermore (1989)** — A novel focused on kender culture, featuring Tas's Uncle Trapspringer.

### Using in Your Campaign

Tasslehoff represents the pure joy of adventure — the character who is just delighted to be here, no matter how dangerous "here" is. A Tas-like NPC can lighten the mood of dark campaigns, provide comic relief during tense moments, and accidentally advance the plot by pocketing exactly the right (or wrong) item. The key is sincerity: Tas is never mean-spirited or deliberately destructive. His chaos comes from genuine enthusiasm and curiosity, not malice. Use kender NPCs sparingly — one Tasslehoff is delightful; a village of them is exhausting. And remember: they do not steal. They just happen to find things.

title: Arkhan the Cruel
summary: A dragonborn paladin/barbarian devoted to Tiamat, Arkhan is a unique character who originated from a celebrity D&D game and became official canon.
content:
## Arkhan the Cruel

**Title/Role:** Champion of Tiamat, Wielder of the Hand of Vecna
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms / Avernus
**Race/Species:** Dragonborn
**Class (if applicable):** Paladin (Oathbreaker) / Barbarian
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil

### Overview

Arkhan the Cruel is a unique figure in D&D — a character who originated as a celebrity player character, created and played by Joe Manganiello (the actor), who was then adopted into official D&D canon. Arkhan is a red dragonborn devoted to Tiamat, the Queen of Evil Dragons, with an unwavering commitment to freeing his goddess from her imprisonment in Avernus. He is a paladin who has broken his oath to good and reforged it in service to the Dragon Queen, combining martial prowess with dark divine magic.

What makes Arkhan compelling is that his devotion to Tiamat is genuine and, in its own twisted way, noble. He does not serve Tiamat for personal gain or out of fear — he truly believes in her cause and is willing to sacrifice everything, including his own humanity, to see her freed. This sincerity makes him a more interesting villain than a simple power-hungry tyrant. He is a fanatic, and fanatics are dangerous precisely because they believe they are righteous.

Arkhan's most dramatic moment came during the finale of a high-profile D&D livestream, when he seized the Hand of Vecna from another character and grafted it to his own arm. This moment was canonized by Wizards of the Coast, and Arkhan now wields the Hand of Vecna in official D&D lore — a dragonborn champion of Tiamat carrying one of the most powerful artifacts in the multiverse. He resides in Avernus near Tiamat's prison, building power and gathering allies for the day he can break her chains.

### Notable Appearances

- **Force Grey: Giant Hunters (2016) / Force Grey: Lost City of Omu (2017)** — The D&D livestream series where Joe Manganiello played Arkhan.
- **Stream of Many Eyes (2018)** — The live event where Arkhan seized the Hand of Vecna, a moment that shocked the D&D community.
- **Baldur's Gate: Descent into Avernus (2019)** — Arkhan appears as an NPC in Avernus, with a stat block and role in the adventure. He has a lair near Tiamat's prison and commands a force of dragonborn and fiendish allies.
- **D&D sourcebooks** — Arkhan's stat block and lore have been included in official materials.

### Using in Your Campaign

Arkhan is an excellent morally complex antagonist for Avernus-set campaigns or any adventure involving Tiamat. He is not mindlessly evil — he can be reasoned with, bargained with, and even allied with if the party's goals temporarily align with Tiamat's interests. The Hand of Vecna adds another layer of danger and intrigue. Use Arkhan as a foil for good-aligned characters, particularly paladins — he demonstrates what happens when divine devotion is directed toward a dark cause. His genuine faith in Tiamat should make the party uncomfortable, because it forces them to confront the fact that conviction alone does not make a cause just.

title: Acquisitions Incorporated
summary: The famous (and infamous) adventuring company turned franchise operation, Acquisitions Incorporated blends corporate satire with dungeon delving in one of D&D's most popular actual-play properties.
content:
## Acquisitions Incorporated

**Title/Role:** Adventuring Company, Franchise Operation
**Setting:** Forgotten Realms (primarily)
**Race/Species:** Various (Organization)
**Class (if applicable):** Various
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good (loosely)

### Overview

Acquisitions Incorporated (Acq Inc) is the most famous adventuring company in modern D&D culture — a group of heroes-for-hire who have transformed dungeon delving from a dangerous vocation into a corporate franchise operation, complete with interns, HR departments, organizational charts, and quarterly earnings reports. What began as a simple actual-play podcast by Penny Arcade creators Jerry Holkins (Omin Dran) and Mike Krahulik (Jim Darkmagic) has grown into a D&D institution with its own official sourcebook, major convention events, and a lasting impact on how people think about adventuring companies in their own games.

The core conceit of Acquisitions Incorporated is the collision of corporate culture with fantasy adventure. Omin Dran, the company's CEO, is a human cleric who views every dungeon as a business opportunity and every quest as a line item on a balance sheet. Jim Darkmagic is a flamboyant wizard from the Darkmagic family of New Hampshire (yes, New Hampshire) who treats adventuring as a performance. The company has employed numerous other adventurers over the years, including the famous "C Team" and various franchise operators.

What makes Acquisitions Incorporated significant beyond entertainment is its influence on D&D culture. It was one of the earliest and most successful D&D actual-play properties, predating Critical Role and helping to demonstrate that watching other people play D&D could be compelling entertainment. The humor is sharp, the characters are memorable, and the adventures — while comedic — often contain genuine dramatic moments and creative problem-solving.

### Notable Appearances

- **Acquisitions Incorporated Podcast (2008-present)** — The original actual-play podcast, one of the first to gain a large audience.
- **PAX Live Games (2010-present)** — Live D&D sessions performed at Penny Arcade Expo conventions, featuring elaborate staging and audience participation.
- **Acquisitions Incorporated sourcebook (2019)** — The official 5th Edition sourcebook that provides rules for running a franchise-style adventuring company, including positions like Cartographer, Decisionist, Hoardsperson, and Occultant.
- **The "C" Team (2017-2020)** — A spin-off series featuring a franchise team of Acquisitions Incorporated.
- **Various D&D crossover events** — Acq Inc characters have appeared at official D&D events and in crossover content.

### Using in Your Campaign

Acquisitions Incorporated provides an excellent framework for campaigns that want to blend humor with adventure. The franchise model from the sourcebook gives parties a built-in organizational structure, complete with headquarters, rivalries with competing franchises, and corporate missions from Head Office. Even without the official rules, the concept of a professional adventuring company provides great campaign structure — the party has a brand to maintain, clients to satisfy, and competitors to outmaneuver. Acq Inc works best in campaigns that embrace D&D's inherent absurdity while still maintaining real stakes and genuine character development.
